item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I absolutely love time traveling and anomalous media scips so this was right up my alley. Has a very nice understated eerieness reminscent of Series 1 yet remains fresh and contemporary while defying reader expectations. Very nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It feels like the method that the communication log used is similar to the method SCP-315 uses.

item.title: Re: An Analysis, I guess.
item.content:encoded:
It means so much to me that you've spent time thinking and theorizing and wondering about something I made. Seriously, as somebody whose writing is rarely read by anyone other than a family member, it makes me feel kinda giddy.

Yeah, maybe I should throw in a sentence or two confirming this, but all the footage is grainy and low-resolution in a manner similar to the format of the original footage. That's because the footage fits itself to the context of the media it's stored on. On the alien planet, where the footage was discovered on a much more intricate piece of technology, the footage is clearer and more defined.

I'm glad you picked up on a bunch of the little patterns I sprinkled throughout, I realized quickly as I was writing this SCP that it would be very number-heavy, so making those numbers significant and meaningful was a goal of mine. Though I will say that the thirteen second thing was just a randomly-chosen number, and you might be giving me more credit than I'm due there lol.

Thanks for the criticisms, too, I should probably make it more clear that people are visible in the apartment footage, and I think I'm just going to outright delete the sentence about it "making up a narrative," it's just too vague and confusing.

I love the Egg! Andy Weir is a great author, I blazed through the Martian audiobook in like a week.

item.title: An Analysis, I guess.
item.content:encoded:
Woah. This was such a crazy and intriguing SCP for me that I want to do a breakdown and commentary of the things that intrigued and stuck out to me the most. Seriously. The imagery in this SCP resonated in my mind for some time, so I felt obliged to let the author see my thoughts.

Feel free to reply back or correct me on anything that I'm talking about.

+ Begin Analysis
- beep

SCP-2335 is a standard, unmarked, commercially-available Memorex 16X DVD-R disk kept within a SuperMedia-S brand DVD case. Basic physical analysis reveals that the disk is of no abnormal composition, and is likely susceptible to corruption in the same manner as one would expect from a relatively inexpensive media disk.

Is this the case for those…things that are also observing whatever is on the data storage disk? Does it also look like a CD for them too?

On a semi-related factor, I'm wondering if all of the footage looks like standard camcorder quality circa 1998 or if the video quality is in HD. If it's the former, then that really ups the creep factor by a lot. The idea of seeing your great-great-great-granddaughter engaging in some unfathomable
recreational activity in a crappy video format is unsettling, given the stark contrast between the past and the future, which makes this even more great.

The same two clips have yet to be repeated, and further investigation suggests that the clips are meant to make up a narrative.

A narrative for whom, I may ask. Probably for the same species of transcendent being that made this DVD, or perhaps even for the creatures in the interview log.

Date and time depicted: [REDACTED]
Events observed: Footage of what appears to be the interior of Facility-███, located in ████████, just minutes before SCP-███ and SCP-████ orchestrate a major containment breach. Researchers and other personnel are documented as they learn of the breach and attempt to prevent or flee it. Notable individuals appearing on the film include Dr. ████████ and Dr. █████, both of whom were reported dead after the breach originally occurred. Despite their efforts, which seem to be the focus of the footage, SCP-████ finds and ████████████████████ Dr. ███████ with its ████████████████████████ resulting in his ████████████████████████████ and eventual death by blood loss. SCP-███ can be seen to ████████████ Dr. █████ fourteen times before he eventually ████████████████████████████████ which lasts for one minute and fourteen seconds.

I like how there's some kind of human element here, even though watching this footage would look horrifying in real life. It's as if the entity was "experimenting."

Date and time depicted: 7/27/2099 9:05:31-9:08:31
Events observed: A crowded arena filled predominantly with teenagers and young adults. Roughly half of the visible individuals are wearing tight blue one-pieces with padded shoulders, the other half wearing red outfits of the same nature. Each individual seems to be holding a glowing yellow baton estimated to be about 14 inches long. It is theorized that the gathering could be part of a mass sport, as the participants seem to be trying to hit others who are wearing the opposite color with the batons. When a baton connects with a vital area, the person hit seems to go limp for an indiscernible period of time. The ultimate goal of the game is unclear given the time of the clip and frame of view, but it seems that members of each team are trying to cross into an "enemy territory" without being incapacitated.

Given that this event is exactly 100 years from the base clip, could this also imply that the arena takes place in the same location? I shudder trying to imagine what kinds of events would have to transpire decades in the future in order for a huge, cosmopolitan arena to be constructed on top of such a tranquil lake.

Date and time depicted: [REDACTED]
Events observed: A ██-second static shot of an apartment complex located in Uptown ██████████. While filmed from too far away to discern any details concerning specific address, photo analysis software has proven that the building is without a doubt the ██████████ Apartments, which happens to be Dr. ████████'s current place of residence.

Were there people depicted here at all, even if they were just people passing by? It said that humans were always depicted in the SCP, so I got a bit confused.

Date and time depicted: 9/08/ ████ 38:13:32-38:14:22
Events observed: A group of humans, arms intertwined, running on completely flat, red terrain. Analysis of the strides of these unidentified humans indicates that they measure between 6'11'' and 7'5'' tall. The humans seem to be completely hairless, and have a reddish tone to their skin unlike that of any known race. They wear skin-tight black pants and colorful bracer-like arm coverings. Each bracer's pattern and color seems to be unique to the individual and crafted of thin metal. There are no distinguishable landscape patterns shown in the frame of the video.

There's some really trippy imagery here. Reading this at first, I admittedly thought of the Goobacks, which made me laugh a little. In a weird future like what's depicted here, people would probably look like this. I'm wondering why the terrain is completely flat, though. It reminds me of SCP-1220, where there was a connection to an alternate reality made entirely of solid clay. It makes me wonder if something like that happened to our reality, and these people are running to escape from the liquefaction effects.

Date and time depicted: [REDACTED]
Events observed: Dr. ████████, head of research on SCP-2335, sleeping in his bed with his wife. The room's lights are on, but the subjects of the footage remain asleep. The camera slowly zooms in on Dr. ████████'s face, and remains there for thirteen seconds before the lights flip off and the clip ends.

Heh, 13 seconds. Either this is a coincidence, or the entity is telling us that he knows about us in a creepy, not-so-subtle manner, like the Mystery Man from Lost Highway.

Date and time depicted: 12/12/1212 12:12:12-12:12:24
Events observed: The clip is roughly twelve seconds long, and consists of a man dressed in > velvet garments reminiscent of an early Italian Barone reaching into a large steaming receptacle > and retrieving a handful of metallic shavings. The shavings appear to be composed of gold. At 12:12:20 the man begins to laugh and throw the shavings in the air.

…Did this guy have a eureka moment with the Philosopher's Stone at exactly 12/12/1212 12:12:12? Why is 12 so significant here? Putting it bluntly, this is some freaky shit. It looks like this being is subliminally telling us that there is a grand design to everything in this universe, for something so significant to happen on this day. Even if the public doesn't know about this accomplishment, this could have easily been important for some other group of interest.

On an unrelated note, this SCP reminds me of a story I read awhile ago called "The Egg" (in a good way, of course),
where a successor to God has to live through the eyes of every person that has every existed throughout time before he can take up the mantle. Similarly, the ending implies that there is a practically unlimited amount of footage here, and that the DVD can theoretically show the lives and actions of every person that has ever existed.

Given that the SCP is called "The Filmmaker," I would have to assume that the being who created or edited this DVD was really involved with the history of humanity, or is really meticulous about documenting humans via his time traveling shenanigans.

Overall, a really creative and great piece: one that had the gears in my head turning for a whole day.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops, I didn't catch that, it's fixed now. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this one! It gave me chills a few times and the interview at the end was really interesting and created more questions in my mind. Great work!

Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is the 9th clip supposed to say that the clip ends at 14:24:36 or 14:25:36?
If it's the former, then we have a serious case of temporal destabilization.

item.title: Re: Small error in the interview.
item.content:encoded: Thanks! That was an artifact from a previous draft that I could have sworn I'd fixed. Good eye.

item.title: Small error in the interview.
item.content:encoded:
Just to let you know OP, you labelled the entity being interviewed as SCP-2335-2, but in the last two lines of the interview, it's labelled SCP-2335-1.

Thought I'd mention it just in case. Good skip btw!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two things that I liked about the interview log: the entity knew very little more than the Foundation, and the use of "there has been". The former avoided the info-dump trope, the latter indicated that the entity's language has certain features that don't parallel English. It did go on a bit before CameraThing pulled the plug, but not enough to spoil the fun.

The sheer variety of the scenes described was very appealing. The depiction of scenes of concern to Foundation personnel was well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending interview got my upvote. This is a decent anomalous media piece, but without any seeming purpose, leaves less of an impact than similar articles. But knowing that someone else has this footage, and doesn't understand it any more than we do, makes this into something wholly unique, without actually explaining anything. Clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll admit, when I saw the footnote, I had high-creep expectations ala SCP-1981. I was mildly disappointed at the lack of scare in that regard, but that was due to my expectations. This is pretty damn good regardless, though.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I like it as is. The aliens not having any more clue either, and the reveal they've been watching it for a long time are definitely worth the clunkiness of the device that gets us there in this case.

ETA: The interview needs quote boxes though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I'm not fond of the interview for the reasons above, I think this skip is otherwise a very strong and suitably creepy one. Not sure if the interview could be repurposed/rewritten to keep the feel of the first half of the article better, though. I'd suggest waiting a little bit more on further feedback, since this is doing well.

I'd also recommend, if you do keep the interview, to implement quote boxes and separate each person speaking. Right now, it looks like one giant block of text, and that instantly makes me drop my attention almost right away.

EDIT: Also, self-upvotes are against the site rules. You can remove it by clicking the "X" on the voting module.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it considered a faux pas to edit the story after it's been posted?

Nah, authors can edit their own works as they see fit.

I would definitely wait to see what other kinds of feedback you get, since this seems to be meeting a positive reception.

Just speaking personally, though, the introduction of both the two-way communication and the alien civilization elements were a little jarring. I kind of feel like there wasn't a whole lot of setup for those things leading up to them, which made it feel like it was coming out of nowhere for me. I would almost prefer no explanation at all to preserve the mystery of the phenomenon, but again, that's just me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback! I really appreciate the comments. I created the interview log hoping that the connection to another, intelligent race with an equally puzzling lack of information about the SCP's origins would heighten the mystery, but I can totally see why that wouldn't be the case.

Is it considered a faux pas to edit the story after it's been posted? If others agreed I could definitely see changing the ending to something more thematically sound.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral voting. The front part of this is great. As a piece of unexplained, anomalous media, it gives off a wonderfully unsettling vibe. In terms of establishing mood, this did a very good job. It hits a very nice sweet spot in the uncanny, vaguely threatening realm of experience.

However, when I saw that there was an interview log, I was apprehensive, and the fact that there's an explanation (even if it's incomplete one) really detracts from this for me. Especially when that explanation undermines suspension of disbelief by then raising questions that would seem to highlight some of the larger points of illogic here.

It's a good effort, but to me it doesn't stick the landing.
